sentences.text: My parents, more or less, tried to protect me from any of the things that were happening.
That was around 1933.
Although I-- I may not have been a particularly avid scholar, but I loved to read the newspapers.
And I did follow the newspapers and I did-- I was aware of the political upheaval that was going on in Germany, the electioneering when Hitler came to power.
So even before the different parties, the different-- the Communist Party, the Centrist Party, the Social Democrats.
And I was aware of these things as young as I was.
I think I started to grow up very quickly and mature very quickly around that time.
And I became very much aware of the political situation.
And when I went to the gymnasium, of course, I was made aware of it very much.
The gymnasium itself-- well, first of all, the, I'd like to say something about my parents' attitude in regards to Nazism.
Of course, when Hitler came to power, the thought was, it's not gonna last, it's gonna blow over.
And my father was a very patriotic German.
He was a veteran of World War I.
He was wounded in World War I.
In fact, he had a piece of shrapnel in his leg till he died.
And he was very proud to serve the kaiser and he was a very proud German and he taught us to be proud Germans, more so than to be proud Jews.
We were made aware that we were Jews.
And we were not religious in any way, at least my parents weren't.
I was more religious.
I went to a Jewish camp at one time, and a very orthodox Jewish camp, and I was almost I can say brainwashed.
And I started to pray twice a day and after each meal.
And I remember my father had ham at the table and I took it once and I threw it out of the window.
[LAUGHS] And he got very upset.
He said, look, if you don't want to eat ham, that's your business.
But you're not going to tell us what to do.

So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- in fact, another thing is I tore toilet paper ahead of time before the Sabbath so I wouldn't have to-- I was really almost fanatically religious for a while, but that subsided.
And-- but I was still very much aware of politically and started to be interested when we were talking about the possible emigration.
And in fact, at first my father said, no, they're not going to touch me, I'm a World War I veteran, I've got the Iron Cross and I'm respected here.
They're not going to touch me.
But soon we found out that that was not to be the case and my parents-- my mother-- at my mother's urging that we made some preparation with the idea of emigrating.
Where to?
I wanted to go to Palestine when the question came and I joined [INAUDIBLE], the Communist youth group.
And my father said, no.
I can't see myself as a farmer and I'm going to try to come to the United States.
In fact, my father came to the United States as a visitor.
I believe it was either 1930-- I think it was 1930-- end of 1935.
I'm not sure as far as the date is concerned.
And he came here with the idea of trying to get an affidavit.
It was the time of the Depression here in the United States and he would have to get a-- a guarantee from someone that we would not become public burdens.

And of course, it was very difficult during the Depression to find someone who would give you a guarantee because here comes somebody who is not trained except as a lawyer, has no other possible means of making a living, no prospects, whose wife has never worked before, he's got a child in school.
That's very risky for someone to give.
And my father was unable to find anyone to give him an affidavit.
He had some names and some people to visit, regards to give, and so on.
And a day or two before he was to come back, unsuccessfully, he visited some-- someone who has a realty business and wanted to give him regards from a friend or far-fetched relative.
And they had a talk and my father said, you know, I'm trying very hard to come to the United States and I was trying to get that affidavit and nobody would give it to me.
So he said, what, nobody would give you the affidavit?
Said, no.
I've exhausted all my possibilities, I have no one else to ask.
Said, well, ?] you didn't ask me.
Said, well, I don't know you.
I'm just here to give you some-- I wouldn't dare to-- to impose upon you.
He said, if you need an affidavit, I'll be happy to give it to you.
And he gave him the affidavit and when he came back, we made plans to emigrate to the United States.
Of course, there was a waiting time, there was a quota.
Only a certain number of people were permitted to enter the United States and we had to wait till the-- our number was called.
And that was not to be until 1937.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, to go back to the gymnasium, the school itself was a very liberal, wonderful school.
We were-- we were taught Latin, French.
We had terrific teachers who were very liberal.
We never had any kind of problems with anti-Semitism until a new teacher came into the school, a young man by the name of Knodt.
K-N-O-D-T.
He was a typical German-- version of a German Aryan-- blond, blue-eyed, tall, handsome-- and he came from [GERMAN], a special school for-- Nazi school for teachers.
And he was assigned to this school.
I don't think anyone had anything to say about it, whether he would be accepted or not.
He was there, that's all I know.
And of all things, he became my class teacher.
And he was-- I must say, he was a very good teacher.
Of course, he propagandized something terrible and there were diatribes against Jews.
And after the first diatribe against Jews, he called me-- there was-- I and another, we were the only Jewish students in that class.
He called us up and he said, if I say something about Jews, I'm not talking about you personally and I'm not talking about your parents.
I'm just talking about Jews in general.
What were we to say about that?
Nothing.
And the diatribes continued and the students became indoctrinated, the students.
And many of them had joined the [GERMAN], which is the junior-- they were too-- we were-- they were too young for the Hitler Youth.
They had the younger-- the Cub Scouts, not the Boy Scouts.
And they came in uniform occasionally, especially when there was holidays or when there were special celebrations to take place, which took place constantly and we were marched around.
And the student-- the attitude of the students changed.
And we were taunted very often, especially on days when they came in uniform, and beaten up very often on the way home.
And I got along fabulously with-- with the-- with the kids.
We had no problems at all.
And when those days came, you were beaten up, the next day they were as nice-- as nice as could be to us again.
And we really couldn't understand that it was like a mass hysteria, like a lynch mentality that took place.
And well, it became a way of life after a while.
And the Jewish students, we sort of stuck together a little bit.
There were not many of us.
And things became rather difficult.
During sporting-- sport events, no one dared to pick us for a team.
And Herr Knodt, he was our gym teacher.
He was very sensitive, actually, to me personally.
I cannot complain about that.
When they refused to pick me, if it's an odd number, he said, well, you join this team here.
They need one more person.
And if it's an even one, he said, I'm going to join you and Werner can join the other team.
And he was really very considerate, considering that he was a very-- a Nazi.

Now, something else came up that was-- that I found out later to be a ruling, that all the Jewish students would have to sit in the last row of the class and would no longer be called upon to-- you know, be called upon.
And this particular teacher, he did it in a way that I didn't even-- wasn't even aware of-- that it happened.
One day, he said, you know, you two, you talk too much.
I want you to come a little closer and I want be able to keep an eye on [? you. ?] So you come over to-- Kahn, you get up and you take their place.
And I was moved about three-- not to the last row, three rows down.
And another student who was not Jewish was moved the same way.
So I thought nothing of it.
Days [INAUDIBLE] later, he called on someone in the last row.
He didn't know the answer to something and he said, you're not paying attention.
I want you to come a little further closer.
I want to be able to keep an eye on you.
Kahn, you go to the-- you change places with him.
I ended up in the last row, thought nothing of it.
And then I noticed that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
--when I raised my hand, he said, Kahn, I know you know the answer.
I want to hear from somebody who I don't think knows the answer as well.
He actually give me a left-handed compliment.
And I thought nothing of it.
And later on I noticed I raised my hand, he never called on me.

One day I visited my aunt and-- and she mentioned something to the effect, can you imagine all the Jewish students in this and this school-- not ours, some other school-- are put in the last row and they don't call on them anymore?
And this and this school they're doing it.
How about you, Werner?
They haven't moved you to the last row.
I said, well, I'm sitting in the last row, but honestly, they moved me into the last-- I was very naive and I thought it was just a natural way I ended up in the last row.
And I must say that teacher was very-- was in retrospect, I have respect for the way he handled it, very sensitively.
But later on, he became a terrible-- he reorganized the school, the teachers.
Some teachers were fired.
I understand later on, he became director of the school.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that was my experience.
And it became- there came a point where my staying-- I wasn't actually thrown out of the school, but there was no point in staying.
Life was made so miserable for me.
I wasn't called on, any kind of class works were either not marked or given back to me with some sort of a stupid remark on it.
So my father took me out of the school and at that time, a Jewish school was formed just for the purpose of students like us.
And some sort of a barracks on the outskirts of the city was taken over for that purpose.
There was no electricity, no heat when we first started.
There was no bell to ring.
And I remember it was a great honor to be made a bell ringer and I was a proud owner of a wrist watch.
And because I had a wrist watch, they made me a bell ringer at one time.
[LAUGHS] For a while, anyway, till someone else complained and got that honorable job, yeah.
Not very long, until our emigration.
Also, my-- my bar mitzvah took place during that time.
Actually, my bar mitzvah was not to be-- was not to be until later on the next year.
But because we were ready to emigrate, it was put ahead.
My family wanted us to be together for that.
I was an only child and of course, my grandparents wanted to be there.
And I remember that my bar mitzvah was the last time we all got together socially.
It was a very enjoyable occasion.
I was prepared for the bar mitzvah by the same teacher who-- religious teacher who prepared-- prepared my father for his bar mitzvah.
And he was not a very well-liked teacher, not even a very good teacher.
But he was an institution and we tolerated-- I was his favorite student for some reason or other.
He says, Werner always carries a book around.
And there was once when I accidentally had a book in my hand.
Of course, after that, I made a point of always carrying books around when I went to religious school, and that impressed him very much.
And behind his back we, of course, we made a lot of fun of that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the gymnasum, while I was there, we also-- we were not hit as much as, but we also-- physical punishment was also par for the course.
And even if we didn't know our vocabulary in Latin or something, we were sort of pummeled and physically hit and so on.
In religious school, things were a little different.
[INAUDIBLE], occasionally he hit us and gave us a [GERMAN], a slap in the face.
But we really must have done something quite badly.

There was another teacher whom we had occasionally in religious school who-- oh, if we didn't do our homework, he yelled at us in class and he told us we had to come to his house as-- to stay after and so on.
And once I was the butt of his wrath and he made me come to his house together with a few others.
And there we had the most wonderful time.
He never mentioned anything about homework or anything.
And his wife came and she served hot chocolate and cake.
And he talked about this and about Jewish history a little bit, anecdotes, stories of Sholem Asch and Sholem Aleichem, and he read to us.
And-- and then we said goodbye.
He said, see to it that you get your homework done the next time I see you.
That was-- I remember.
And it was all the way at the other end of town.
I had to take my bike and travel there.
But to get back to the bar mitzvah again, which was just weeks before we left for the States, and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Well, we were still able to take furniture out at that time-- no money, but all our furniture.
I remember my bicycle went into a lift and our furniture went into a lift.
And in fact, my parents bought some pots and pans and things.
And we traveled-- really-- come to think of it now, it's really very incongruous.
We traveled as refugees first class on a-- on a ship going over in the lap of luxury.
Because we had money in Germany, but we couldn't bring it over.
So my father said, well, might as well spend it on-- on expensive tickets.
And when we arrived here, people couldn't understand that people came over here practically penniless and they traveled first class.
And it was very hard to explain to Americans here.
Preparation, I don't remember any other preparations except a few hasty English lessons by a student, just a few words of English.
In gymnasium, I did study French and Latin.
I was in the second grade, [NON-ENGLISH], when I left, and that was equivalent of-- what is it-- fifth, sixth grade, I guess.
And I continued and we didn't learn any English in the Hebrew school, in the religious school-- in the-- in the Jewish school.
We just learned [? general ?] things and practical things like farming.
Because they told us there was a little plot of earth and we planted some radishes and-- and lettuce because many of the people had plans to go to Palestine at the time.
And there they would have become farmers most likely.
So a little bit of practical work, which was a good thing, actually.
Shop, carpentry, a few practical things.
So that's-- otherwise, I don't-- the lessons were very general because many grades were put together.
And it's a little difficult to teach in a two-room school that have many different grades.
Oh, it was a great adventure for me.
And I looked at the whole thing-- of course, it was different for my parents.
They were very sad and their-- they didn't know what the future would hold for them.
And I was looking forward to it.
I was sorry in a way that we weren't coming to Palestine, but my mother always said, can you imagine your father as a farmer?
You must be out of your mind.
[LAUGHS] And there was nothing else.
They said, it's going to be difficult enough coming to the States and-- to start an existence.
And-- but to me, there was nothing to worry about.
I was kind of naive, of course.
I knew politically what was going on, but I had no inkling of the struggle that my parents had coming to this country, to the States, once they got here.
And you know, you always read stories, oh, in the States everything is-- the streets are paved with gold and things are wonderful there.
And we-- only rarely did we actually hear of people struggling.
And the attitude was, well, it was probably their fault.
They didn't try hard enough.
But we found that things weren't-- when we came, it was in the midst of the Depression in 1937.
There was a tremendous amount of unemployment here and the only jobs available were jobs that nobody else would take, unskilled jobs.
Oh, there's another funny story about that.
My father always told me, he said, you know, you'll be hardly over the gangplank and people will ask you, how do you like America?
And he said, the answer always, just fine, thank you.
And that was his experience when he was over here as a visitor.
And I remember we landed in Hoboken.
Before that, we passed the Statue of Liberty.
It was early in the morning.

My father took me up to the deck and he told me about the Statue of Liberty, how-- who built it and what it stood for and that it-- it-- it was the symbol of the United States, more or less, and that everyone has the highest regard and respect for liberty and the Statue of Liberty being the symbol of liberty.
And after that, we landed in Hoboken and some friends who had been there a few months earlier also from Wiesbaden came.
Actually, the lady was a very good friend of my mother's.
And they came and there was an American friend also who came.
And I remember we landed in Hoboken next to the old Leviathan-- there was a rusty old wreck.
It was an old ship that was called a different name but it wasn't used anymore.
It used to be the pride of the-- it was a reparation from World War I.
[INAUDIBLE], no not the [INAUDIBLE].
It had some sort of a name.
And we went over the gangplank and then American friend, the first question-- the first thing I heard was, how do you like America [LAUGHS] as I walked over the gangplank.
And I said, just fine, just fine.
And then they took me to a drugstore, where I had the-- I was supposed to try an ice cream soda.
It was a very seedy-- looking drugstore.
They said, that's the national drink.
I thought it was the most horrible drink imaginable.
But of course, they meant well.
I said, oh, it was delicious.
And that was my first day coming into Hoboken.
And we settled in Washington Heights, in New York, which was more or less the German-- most of the German refugees settled there.
It was inexpensive, it was a relatively decent neighborhood at that time.
And we had a room-- they rented a room for us in a boarding house where the three of us lived in one room for the first six months or so.
And every room was occupied by a refugee looking for work.
And at dinnertime, we all sat around the table and the conversation always was about jobs, the looking for jobs.
There was somebody who became a butcher and he had no idea about being a butcher.
He applied at six different butchers and he was-- after one day, he said he was experienced, he was thrown out.
And after the six ones, he knew enough to remain hired.
And later on, I had a very similar experience myself when I first became a photographer.
[LAUGHS] But that's-- and one former I think he was an accountant, a bookkeeper over there, he became an auto mechanic.
And somebody else-- they all took really menial jobs, whatever they could find.
He looked for a job and it took a long time.
He couldn't find anything, of course.
But finally, he took whatever he could find and it was-- the first job that he got was selling razor blades to candy stores.
And he went to a wholesaler, he got one of those cardboard racks and-- where the razor blades were stapled on there.
And he went from razor-- from store to store peddling razor blades.
